statement.en: Polyunsaturated fatty acids mediate the antitumor effects of fasting by inducing lipid peroxidation and ferroptosis in breast cancer
statement.summary: Published preclinical studies showed that cyclic fasting or fasting-mimicking diets (FMDs) delay tumour progression and enhance survival in murine models of breast cancer. Our lipidomic analyses revealed that both fasting and FMDs lead to elevated plasma levels of the polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) arachidonic acid (AA) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) in mice and in patients with breast cancer. Gene set enrichment analysis of transcriptomic profiles from breast cancer samples of patients subjected to cyclic FMD showed a pronounced activation of fatty acid metabolism–related pathways. Here, we show that the anticancer effects of fasting are rescued if systemic lipolysis is inhibited and plasma fatty acid increase during fasting is prevented. reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: Paper dated 2026-04-25 is a future date (beyond current knowledge cutoff April 2024); presented as preclinical/translational research with lipidomic and transcriptomic evidence in murine models and human patients, but mechanistic claims about ferroptosis induction require independent replication and clinical validation. The abstract is incomplete (cuts mid-sentence at 'supplementation of an excess'), indicating provisional or preprint status.
